splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuit courts disagree on whether ERISA requires actuarial assumptions used to calculate an employer's withdrawal liability from a multiemployer pension plan to be locked in by the end of the prior plan year. This affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers participating in multiemployer pension plans.

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that the actuary may select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, as the statute does not require assumptions to be fixed by year-end.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions must be determined as of the statutory measurement date (end of the prior plan year), providing employers greater predictability about potential liability.

circuits.name: Sixth Circuit
circuits.caseName: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuit courts split on whether federal courts may apply a judicially-created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending court-ordered conditions beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. This affects thousands of individuals who abscond from supervised release each year.

positions.label: Tolling applies
positions.summary: Holds that the term of supervised release is tolled when a supervisee absconds, so the supervised release period does not run during the time the person is a fugitive.

positions.label: Tolling does not apply
positions.summary: Holds that there is no statutory basis for tolling supervised release when a supervisee absconds, and courts lack authority to create such a doctrine.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: A longstanding 6-to-3 circuit split exists on whether federal prisoners face a categorical bar under § 2244(b)(1) from re-raising claims in successive § 2255 motions. If (b)(1) applies, federal prisoners cannot raise any claim they previously presented; if not, they need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s requirements of newly discovered evidence or a new constitutional law rule.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that the gatekeeping bar of § 2244(b)(1), which categorically prohibits relitigating previously raised claims, applies to federal prisoners filing successive § 2255 motions.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1) does not apply to federal prisoners' successive § 2255 motions, and such prisoners need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s own gatekeeping requirements.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuit courts disagree on whether candidates have standing to bring pre-election challenges to voting procedures. Some circuits recognize competitive standing for candidates challenging election rules, while others require a more concrete showing of injury. The outcome affects the ability to challenge mail-in voting rules, ballot counting deadlines, and other election administration practices.

positions.label: Candidate has standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates have Article III standing to challenge election rules that allegedly dilute the value of lawful votes, recognizing competitive injury as sufficient.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

positions.label: Candidate lacks standing
positions.summary: Holds that a candidate's generalized interest in fair elections, without a concrete and particularized injury, is insufficient to establish Article III standing for pre-election challenges.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify a defendant) or disjunctive (any one criterion is disqualifying).
splits.description: A deep circuit split exists on whether a defendant must satisfy all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1) to be disqualified from safety-valve relief, or whether possessing any one criterion is disqualifying. This affects whether district courts must impose sentences without regard to statutory minimums and creates disparate sentencing across the country for defendants with identical criminal histories.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), reading 'and' in its ordinary conjunctive sense.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1) is sufficient to disqualify a defendant from safety-valve relief.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Sentencing Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' in U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2(b) includes inchoate offenses such as conspiracy and attempt, based on Application Note 1's expansion of the guideline text.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2, which adds inchoate offenses like conspiracy to the definition of 'controlled substance offense,' is a valid authoritative interpretation under Stinson v. United States or impermissibly expands the guideline text. This affects career offender designations and sentence lengths for many defendants.

positions.label: Application Note 1 is valid — inchoate offenses included
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1's inclusion of conspiracy, aiding and abetting, and attempt in the definition of 'controlled substance offense' is an authoritative interpretation of the guideline text entitled to deference under Stinson.

positions.label: Application Note 1 is invalid — inchoate offenses excluded
positions.summary: Holds that the guideline text of § 4B1.2(b) is unambiguous and does not include inchoate offenses, and Application Note 1 impermissibly expands the definition beyond what the text allows.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2(b) when the state-controlled substance is not listed on the federal controlled substances schedules.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' requires the underlying substance to be federally scheduled, or whether any state-law drug offense qualifies regardless of federal scheduling. This affects career offender and other sentencing enhancements for defendants with prior state drug convictions.

positions.label: State offenses qualify regardless of federal scheduling
positions.summary: Holds that § 4B1.2(b) broadly includes any 'offense under state law' and there is no textual basis to graft a federal-scheduling limitation onto the career offender guideline.

positions.label: State offenses must involve federally scheduled substances
positions.summary: Holds that a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' only if the underlying substance is also controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a non-retroactive change in sentencing law, such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c), can constitute an 'extraordinary and compelling reason' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply divided on whether non-retroactive changes in sentencing law — alone or in combination with other factors — can support a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release. Some circuits categorically bar reliance on such changes, while others allow them as one factor. The Sentencing Commission's 2024 policy statement amendment (§ 1B1.13(b)(6)) attempted to allow this, but some circuits hold the amendment exceeds the Commission's authority.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in law, including the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment, can be considered as one factor among others in evaluating whether extraordinary and compelling reasons exist for a sentence reduction.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in law cannot contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for sentence reduction under § 3582(c)(1)(A), whether offered alone or in combination with other factors.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether federal prisoners challenging conditions of confinement or BOP decisions affecting the terms of their imprisonment must proceed via habeas corpus or may bring claims under civil rights statutes such as Bivens or the APA.
splits.description: A longstanding circuit split exists on whether habeas corpus is the exclusive remedy when prisoners challenge BOP decisions that affect the execution of their sentences (such as early release credits or transfer to prerelease custody), or whether such challenges may proceed as civil rights or APA actions. The split has persisted for over half a century.

positions.label: Habeas is the exclusive remedy for challenges affecting duration
positions.summary: Holds that when a prisoner challenges a BOP decision that could result in earlier release, habeas corpus is the exclusive federal remedy and civil rights actions are unavailable.

positions.label: Both habeas and civil rights actions are available
positions.summary: Holds that prisoners may bring civil rights or APA actions to challenge BOP decisions affecting the conditions or terms of confinement, even when the challenge may also affect release timing.

circuits.name: Third Circuit
circuits.caseName: Woodall v. Federal Bureau of Prisons

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may expressly punish a defendant for the underlying violation of supervised release conditions when imposing a revocation sentence, or whether revocation sentences must be based solely on the breach of the court's trust.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether a sentencing court revoking supervised release may impose a sentence that punishes the defendant for the conduct constituting the violation, or whether the sentence must be limited to sanctioning the breach of trust inherent in violating release conditions. The Sixth Circuit allows express punishment for violations, placing it at the extreme of a split with circuits that limit the revocation sentence's purpose.

positions.label: Courts may punish for the underlying violation
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may expressly punish defendants for the conduct underlying their supervised release violations when imposing revocation sentences.

positions.label: Revocation sentences must address breach of trust, not punishment for violations
positions.summary: Holds that supervised release revocation sentences should be based on the breach of the defendant's relationship with the court, not as additional punishment for the underlying violative conduct.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of a debtor's property belong to the bankruptcy estate or to the debtor when a Chapter 13 case is converted in good faith to Chapter 7.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A) allows debtors who convert from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 to retain post-petition appreciation in their property. The Ninth Circuit holds that appreciation belongs to the estate, while other courts hold that it belongs to the debtors. This significantly affects the incentive structure for debtors in reorganization.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that post-petition, pre-conversion increases in property equity inure to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7, based on the broad scope of § 541(a).

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that under § 348(f)(1)(A), debtors who convert in good faith from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 retain post-petition appreciation in their property, as the estate property is measured as of the original petition date.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the time limit to appeal a Hyde Amendment order for attorney's fees is governed by the civil appeal deadline (30 days under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a)) or the criminal appeal deadline (14 days under Fed. R. App. P. 4(b)).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees in a criminal case is a civil or criminal matter for purposes of determining the applicable appeal deadline. The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies because the motion is ancillary to the criminal case, but a minority of circuits apply the criminal deadline.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies (30 days)
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, and the 30-day civil appeal deadline under Rule 4(a) applies.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies (14 days)
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion arises from and is part of the criminal case, and the shorter criminal appeal deadline under Rule 4(b) applies.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court order denying a motion to seal documents containing private medical information is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine.
splits.description: The D.C. Circuit held that orders denying motions to seal medical records qualify for immediate appellate review under the collateral order doctrine because medical privacy is a 'value of high order.' Other circuits have taken varying approaches to whether sealing denials are immediately appealable, creating tension over the scope of the collateral order doctrine.

positions.label: Immediately appealable under collateral order doctrine
positions.summary: Holds that denying a motion to seal medical information implicates a value of sufficiently high order to warrant immediate appellate review because delayed review risks irreversible disclosure.

positions.label: Not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that orders denying motions to seal are not effectively unreviewable on appeal from final judgment and therefore do not satisfy the collateral order doctrine's requirements.

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of discrimination or unfair treatment in the operation of hospitality or business establishments under 42 U.S.C. § 1981 or related civil rights statutes?
splits.description: The Supreme Court granted certiorari to review the Second Circuit's approach to discrimination claims in hospitality settings, indicating a circuit split on the applicable legal standards for such claims. The case will clarify standards for fair treatment, discrimination liability, and damages in service industries nationwide.

positions.label: Second Circuit approach
positions.summary: Applied a particular legal framework to discrimination claims arising from hospitality establishments that the Supreme Court found sufficiently unsettled to warrant review.

positions.label: Differing approach by other circuits
positions.summary: Other circuits have applied different standards to discrimination claims in commercial or hospitality contexts, creating the split that prompted SCOTUS review.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: Circuits are divided on whether the FAAAA's broad preemption clause, which bars states from enacting or enforcing laws 'related to a price, route, or service of any... broker,' preempts common-law negligence claims against freight brokers arising from accidents involving the motor carriers they selected. This affects liability standards across the trucking and logistics industry.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state negligence claims against freight brokers are preempted because broker selection of carriers is a 'service' related to the arrangement of transportation.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Miller v. C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers for negligent selection of carriers are not preempted by the FAAAA because they do not relate to brokers' prices, routes, or services as those terms are properly construed.

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3), which prohibits unlawful users of controlled substances from possessing firearms, violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.
splits.description: Following the Supreme Court's decision in New York State Rifle & Pistol Ass'n v. Bruen, circuits have split on whether § 922(g)(3)'s prohibition on firearm possession by users of controlled substances, particularly marijuana, is consistent with the Second Amendment's text and the nation's historical tradition of firearms regulation.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is constitutional
positions.summary: Holds that the historical tradition of disarming those who pose a danger to themselves or others, including habitual substance users, supports the constitutionality of § 922(g)(3) even after Bruen.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is unconstitutional as applied
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because the government cannot demonstrate a sufficient historical analogue for disarming non-violent drug users.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether compassionate release motions may serve as a vehicle for raising claims that could also be brought under § 2255 habeas petitions, such as claims of legal error at sentencing. Some circuits permit consideration of such factors as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis, while others hold that § 3582(c)(1)(A) may not be used to circumvent § 2255's procedural requirements.

positions.label: May consider conviction/sentence errors
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider claims related to errors in conviction or sentencing as one factor in the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

positions.label: May not consider conviction/sentence errors
positions.summary: Holds that compassionate release under § 3582(c)(1)(A) may not be used to raise claims of legal error that are properly the subject of § 2255 motions, as this would circumvent habeas procedural requirements.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether workers who distribute baked goods to retail stores qualify as 'transportation workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, thereby exempting them from mandatory arbitration agreements.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on the scope of the Section 1 exemption for transportation workers, particularly whether last-mile delivery workers who distribute goods to retail stores are 'engaged in . . . interstate commerce.' This affects the enforceability of arbitration agreements for a wide range of distribution workers.

positions.label: Broad exemption — last-mile delivery workers qualify
positions.summary: Holds that workers who are part of a continuous interstate transportation chain, including those making last-mile deliveries of goods that have moved in interstate commerce, qualify as transportation workers exempt from the FAA.

positions.label: Narrow exemption — last-mile distributors do not qualify
positions.summary: Holds that distribution workers delivering goods locally to retail stores are not 'transportation workers engaged in . . . interstate commerce' under Section 1 because they are not themselves moving goods across state lines.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims based on allegedly inadequate product labeling when the EPA has not required the specific warning.
splits.description: The circuits disagree on whether FIFRA's preemption provision bars state-law failure-to-warn claims against pesticide manufacturers when the claims are based on labeling that the EPA approved. This affects product liability litigation involving agricultural chemicals and consumer pesticides nationwide.

positions.label: FIFRA preempts failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims that would require labeling different from or in addition to what the EPA has approved.

positions.label: FIFRA does not preempt failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA does not preempt state-law failure-to-warn claims because they impose requirements 'parallel to' rather than 'in addition to' FIFRA's labeling requirements, consistent with the Supreme Court's Bates v. Dow Agrosciences framework.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Monsanto Company v. John L. Durnell

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Religious Land Use and Institutionalized Persons Act (RLUIPA) authorizes individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether RLUIPA creates a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials in their individual capacities. Some circuits have held that RLUIPA, enacted under the Spending Clause, does not clearly authorize personal-liability damages against individuals who are not direct recipients of federal funds, while others have allowed such claims to proceed.

positions.label: RLUIPA authorizes individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA provides a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials sued in their individual capacities for violations of prisoners' religious exercise rights.

positions.label: RLUIPA does not authorize individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA does not clearly authorize individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials because Congress cannot impose personal liability on non-recipient individuals through the Spending Clause.

circuits.name: Fifth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Sossamon v. Lone Star State of Texas

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Landor v. Louisiana Department of Corrections and Public Safety

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 47(b) of the Investment Company Act creates a private right of action allowing parties to a contract that violates the ICA to sue in federal court for rescission of that contract.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether § 47(b) of the ICA provides an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts made in violation of the Act. This affects the ability of private parties to enforce the ICA's protections against abusive investment company practices through direct federal court actions.

positions.label: Section 47(b) creates a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) creates an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts that violate the Investment Company Act, allowing private parties to sue directly.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

positions.label: Section 47(b) does not create a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) is a voidability provision rather than a private right of action, and only the SEC can enforce the ICA's prohibitions.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, as construed under the Gingles framework, can constitutionally compel race-based redistricting as a remedy for vote dilution, and whether compliance with Section 2 constitutes a compelling governmental interest justifying the use of race in drawing district lines.
splits.description: This case raises the question of whether race-conscious redistricting mandated by Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act is constitutionally permissible under the Equal Protection Clause. Lower courts have applied the Gingles framework to require consideration of race, but the constitutionality of this framework as applied is increasingly contested.

positions.label: Section 2 compels constitutionally permissible race-conscious redistricting
positions.summary: Holds that compliance with Section 2 of the VRA, as interpreted under the Gingles framework, constitutes a compelling interest that justifies the use of race in drawing district lines.

positions.label: Section 2's compelled race-based redistricting raises constitutional concerns
positions.summary: Holds or suggests that the Gingles framework's requirement of race-based redistricting may exceed constitutional limits under the Equal Protection Clause, particularly after Dobbs-era equal protection jurisprudence.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Arkansas State Conference of the NAACP v. Arkansas Board of Apportionment
